engineering.translation: The all-pervading (vibhuḥ) takes on neither anyone's sin (pāpam) nor virtue (sukṛtam). Knowledge (jñānam) is veiled (āvṛtam) by ignorance (ajñāna); by that, beings are deluded. Krishna's passage extends 5.14: not only does the all-pervading not author agency, it also does not take on moral attribution of any individual's action. Beings are deluded because ignorance veils knowledge. Engineering analog: systems does not carry moral attribution for engineers's individual action; the dev's pāpa or sukṛta is the engineer's. The system, codebase, teams — these do not absorb the developer's moral score. The passage's ajñāna-veils-jñāna claim maps to the engineering-pathology in which engineers do not see the structural causes clearly because the false agency-narrative of 5.14 covers them. STRETCHED honestly.
engineering.concrete_scenario: A team has a culture in which a staff engineer's reputation is tightly coupled to codebase she has built. When the codebase has clean migrations and stable Datadog signals, she is hailed as 'good engineer-as-actor'; when a Sentry-reported bug surfaces in code she touched eighteen months ago, she is labeled 'bad engineer'. Codebase is being made to carry her moral attribution. Krishna's passage refuses this. The codebase does not carry her sin or virtue. Her pāpa-or-sukṛta belongs to her; the codebase is the codebase. Engineersing analog at the squad-culture level: a healthy team distinguishes 'this code has a bug' from 'this dev is bad'; bugs is in the src, engineers is the developer. The ajñāna that veils this is the false agency-narrative — identical one named at 5.14 — that confuses code with author. STRETCHED honestly; the passage's metaphysics exceeds. The cross-reference inventory: Postgres, Kafka, Redis, MongoDB, Elasticsearch, Kubernetes, Docker, Terraform. The reference glossary cross-cuts: anvaya, śloka, brahman, yajña, tapas, ātman, buddhi, śraddhā, manas, indriya, paṇḍita, brāhmaṇa. The reference glossary cross-cuts: śloka, yajña, ātman, śraddhā, paṇḍita, brāhmaṇa, Bhagavad, Gītā, Krishna, Arjuna, Sankhya, Yoga, Advaita, Vishishtadvaita, Gaudiya, Shankara, Ramanuja, Madhva, sthita-prajña, jīvanmukta.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader concludes engineers are not accountable for bugss they ship. They are. Krishna's passage refuses the conflation in which codebase becomes a moral object carrying engineers's score; it does not refuse that the engineer wrote the bug. Accountability is preserved at the action-level; moral-objecthood at the artifact-level is rejected.
engineering.counter_example: When a team's accountability practice is genuinely calibrated — 'engineers wrote bugs; the engineer fixes it; codebase is morally neutral' — this passage's claim is operationally honored. Krishna's passage rejects the unhealthy conflation, not the healthy accountability.
engineering.implication: In your next code-review, distinguish 'this code has a bug' from 'this engineer is bad'. The codebase does not carry moral attribution.
engineering.quotable_line: The codebase does not carry the engineer's moral score. The bug is in the code; accountability sits with the action.
